metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving step for AC7: specify at least one integration test that, with `mcp.auth.enabled=false`, asserts an unauthenticated request returns 200 (not 401) and no `WWW-Authenticate` header is present, and that the `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` endpoint is absent (404).
findings.finding: AC7 ('With mcp.auth.enabled=false the server behaves exactly as S1 (auth middleware absent)') has no described test step or proving command. The outcome 'behaves exactly as S1' is also not fully operationalised — the only clarification is the parenthetical '(auth middleware absent)', but no test asserts this concretely.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC7 bullet with: 'Integration test asserts that with `mcp.auth.enabled=false`: an unauthenticated request returns 200 (no 401), no `WWW-Authenticate` header is present, and `GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` returns 404 (observable oracle: status codes + header absence).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete config-plumbing approach for `[mcp.auth]`: either (a) flatten auth fields directly into `McpConfig` + add them to `_SECTIONS['mcp']`, or (b) introduce a new top-level `auth` section (new `AuthConfig` dataclass, new `_SECTIONS['auth']` entry, new `_SECTION_CLASSES['auth']` entry, new `Config.auth` field) — and name all the parallel edit targets explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `[mcp.auth]` extends `McpConfig` by 'mirroring the existing `readonly`/`allow_llm` gate config pattern', but the existing `McpConfig` is a flat 3-field dataclass (lines 375-378, `_config_schema.py`) and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (lines 513-517) only registers flat scalar coercers. The `coerce_sparse` function has NO nested-table/sub-section dispatch path. A `[mcp.auth]` TOML sub-table is not a mirror of the existing pattern — it requires either a new top-level `[mcp_auth]` section (with its own `_SECTIONS` + `_SECTION_CLASSES` entries and a new `Config` field), a flat approach that packs auth fields directly into `McpConfig`, or a bespoke nested-coercer extension none of which the plan specifies.
findings.location: What section — 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; Approach — 'mirror the existing `readonly`/`allow_llm` gate config pattern already in `McpConfig`'
findings.suggested_fix: Given the coercer's flat-section-only architecture, the path of least resistance is option (a): flatten the auth fields directly into `McpConfig` (e.g., `auth_enabled: bool`, `auth_strategies: tuple[str, ...]`, `auth_issuer_url: str`, etc.) and register each in `_SECTIONS['mcp']` with the appropriate coercer. The TOML shape would then be `[mcp]` with `auth_enabled = true` rather than `[mcp.auth]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `[mcp.auth]` is implemented in the coercer: either flatten all auth keys into the `[mcp]` section (e.g., `mcp.auth_enabled`), add a new top-level `[mcp_auth]` section, or extend `coerce_sparse` to handle nested TOML sub-tables.
findings.finding: A nested TOML `[mcp.auth]` sub-table is structurally incompatible with the existing flat-coercer architecture in `coerce_sparse`; the plan names it as a config group but doesn't specify how the coercer handles it.
findings.location: What section — 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` lines 556-595 (`coerce_sparse`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Redesign the [mcp.auth] config mechanism to be compatible with the flat coerce_sparse architecture — either (a) adopt the _RESERVED_SECTIONS/read_reserved_section pattern already used by 'llm' and 'snapshot' (allows mcp_auth.py to own its own raw-dict parsing of nested structures like static_tokens), or (b) flatten all auth keys directly into McpConfig with auth_ prefixes and handle static_tokens via env-only injection — and update the plan's What and Approach sections to name the chosen mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan's '[mcp.auth]' nested sub-group mechanism is structurally incompatible with the real config schema architecture: _config_schema.py's coerce_sparse handles only flat section→key mappings (one level), has no support for sub-tables within a section, and _emit_toml explicitly raises ConfigError on nested sub-tables. The static_tokens field (a list-of-tables with client_id, scopes, and token_env/token_sha256 per entry) is also outside what the existing scalar/list coercers can represent. The plan's stated approach of extending McpConfig 'following the existing coercer pattern' cannot produce the [mcp.auth] sub-group or serialize static_tokens without a structural redesign of the config layer.
findings.location: What section ('Extend McpConfig with an [mcp.auth] group') / Approach section ('mirror the existing readonly/allow_llm gate config pattern already in McpConfig') / src/rebar/_config_schema.py lines 374-378 (McpConfig), 513-517 (_SECTIONS['mcp']), 556-595 (coerce_sparse)
findings.suggested_fix: Use the _RESERVED_SECTIONS / read_reserved_section pattern: add 'mcp_auth' to _RESERVED_SECTIONS, give _mcp_auth.py a McpAuthConfig.from_raw(raw_dict) class method that parses the nested structure (including static_tokens list-of-tables), and have build_server call rebar.config.read_reserved_section('mcp_auth') then pass to the factory. This is exactly how the llm layer handles complex optional config and requires no changes to the core coercer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the parsing/validation protocol for the `static_tokens` list-of-tables: name the module responsible (config schema coercer vs. `_mcp_auth.py` load-time), define how sub-record fields are validated, and clarify whether/how `REBAR_MCP_*` env-var derivation applies to this structured field.
findings.finding: The `static_tokens` list-of-tables is a nested, variable-length structure (list of records, each with multi-field sub-schema) that cannot be handled by the existing flat `coerce_sparse` coercer pattern (verified: `_SECTIONS['mcp']` at `_config_schema.py:513-517` maps `mcp` keys to scalar coercers only). No protocol is specified for how this nested structure is parsed, validated, and coerced through the config pipeline, leaving the decision rule for how to parse it unco-located with the schema that needs it.
findings.location: What section — '`static_tokens` schema' paragraph; AC1

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `[mcp.auth]` config can be added as a nested sub-group inside `McpConfig` following the 'existing coercer pattern', but the codebase shows `McpConfig` is a flat 3-field dataclass (`readonly`, `allow_llm`, `allow_jira_sync`) and `coerce_sparse` processes each top-level section as a flat key→value dict dispatched via `_SECTIONS['mcp']` — there is no nested sub-table mechanism in the current infrastructure. Adding `[mcp.auth]` as a TOML sub-group would silently be ignored (unknown key warning) or raise a ConfigError without structural changes to coerce_sparse, _SECTION_CLASSES, and McpConfig.
findings.location: What section — '[mcp.auth] group' / src/rebar/_config_schema.py:375-378 and 513-517
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) introduce auth fields as flat `mcp.*` keys (e.g., `mcp.auth_enabled`, `mcp.auth_strategies`) mirroring the existing pattern — requiring only field additions to McpConfig and coercer entries in _SECTIONS['mcp'] — or (b) add a new top-level `[auth]` section with its own `AuthConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['auth']` entry, and update `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `Config` accordingly. Option (a) is the path of least resistance given the existing infra.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `[mcp.auth]` can be added as a nested sub-group inside `McpConfig` following 'the existing coercer pattern', but the existing `McpConfig` is a flat 3-field dataclass and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` is a flat key→coercer dict. The `coerce_sparse` function iterates `_SECTIONS[sect]` for scalar coercers only; no mechanism exists for a nested sub-table within a section. Other nested configs (e.g., `llm`) use the `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` escape hatch rather than the coercer pipeline. The plan never acknowledges this structural gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) flatten the auth fields into `McpConfig` (e.g., `auth_enabled`, `auth_strategies`, …) and register them in `_SECTIONS['mcp']` as scalar coercers, matching the existing pattern exactly; or (b) add `'mcp_auth'` as a new top-level `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS` entry with its own coercer dict, and use TOML `[tool.rebar.mcp_auth]`. Either path requires updating the plan's AC1 to name the exact coercer registration.

findings.finding: The plan names `[mcp.auth]` as a TOML sub-table (a nested group under `[mcp]`) but the config infrastructure — `McpConfig` (a flat dataclass), `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (flat key→coercer dict), and `coerce_sparse` (processes only top-level sections) — has no mechanism to parse nested sub-sections. The plan never enumerates `_SECTIONS['mcp']` and `_SECTION_CLASSES` in `_config_schema.py` as edit targets, nor does it specify how the nested `[mcp.auth]` TOML sub-table will be parsed (new top-level section, nested dataclass + sub-section coercer, or flat keys under `[mcp]`).
findings.location: plan section 'What': 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:375-378`, `478-517`, `462-475`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `_SECTIONS['mcp']` and `_SECTION_CLASSES` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as explicit parallel edit targets when extending `McpConfig` with new auth fields.
findings.finding: `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (the coercer dispatch table at `_config_schema.py` lines 513-517) and `_SECTION_CLASSES` (line 462-475) are mandatory parallel edit targets whenever `McpConfig` fields change, but the plan never names them as edit targets.
findings.location: What section — 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` lines 462-475, 513-517
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Register each new `mcp.auth.*` key in `_SECTIONS["mcp"]` with the appropriate coercer lambda (e.g., `_as_bool` for `enabled`, `_as_str_tuple` for `strategies`, `_as_str` for `issuer_url`/`resource_server_url`/`required_scopes`) and add the section class to `_SECTION_CLASSES`.' Also address whether auth fields are nested under an `auth` sub-key or flattened into the `mcp` section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Approach section to reference the _RESERVED_SECTIONS/read_reserved_section pattern (as used by 'llm' and 'snapshot') as the correct codebase model for mcp_auth config, explaining why it fits better than the flat gate-bool pattern for nested/complex auth config.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a full CompositeTokenVerifier + strategies config key + factory infrastructure in S2 when only one verifier (StaticBearerVerifier) exists at delivery time and no alternative approach for the existing reserved-section config pattern is considered. The _RESERVED_SECTIONS/read_reserved_section pattern (used for 'llm' and 'snapshot') is a structurally different, clearly-superior alternative for auth config: it already handles complex optional nested config (like static_tokens list-of-tables), avoids rewriting the flat coercer, and has a smaller blast radius. The plan's positive rationale ('mirror the existing readonly/allow_llm gate config pattern') points to the WRONG existing pattern for this data shape.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('mirror the existing readonly/allow_llm gate config pattern already in McpConfig')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the canonical strategy identifier string for the static bearer verifier (e.g. `"static_bearer"`) in the plan, and note that S3–S6 must register their own identifiers against the same factory; add an example `strategies` value to the `[mcp.auth]` config spec.
findings.finding: The valid string value(s) for the `strategies` list that activate the static bearer verifier are never named in the plan. An executor building the config→verifier factory must invent an identifier (e.g. `"static_bearer"`) with no authoritative reference, creating a risk of inconsistency across stories S3–S6 that will add more strategy names to the same list.
findings.location: What section — `strategies` (ordered list selecting which verifiers are active); Approach section — 'config→verifier factory'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The factory recognises `"static_bearer"` for `StaticBearerVerifier`; future stories register `"jwks_jwt"`, `"introspection"`, `"trusted_proxy"`, `"custom"` respectively. Example: `strategies = ["static_bearer"]`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile audience-validation semantics: either (a) specify that wrong-audience causes an immediate composite-level deny (raise or short-circuit, not None) and update AC5's oracle from 'returns None' to 'raises / causes immediate denial', or (b) explicitly state that audience is enforced at the composite level before dispatching to any verifier, and update AC5 accordingly.
findings.finding: The audience-validation behavior is contradictory between the Security/What sections and AC5's observable oracle. The Security section states 'audience validated on every request' as a hard deny; the What section says a rejected token means 'not my token' (None) and the composite tries the next verifier. AC5's observable oracle says 'a wrong-audience token is rejected (returns None)' — but in the composite's own contract, returning None means fall-through to the next verifier, not an immediate denial. An implementer following AC5 literally would return None from StaticBearerVerifier on wrong-audience, potentially allowing another verifier to accept the same token.
findings.location: AC5 (unit test — wrong-audience) vs. 'What' composite contract vs. 'Security' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the composite contract, add: 'audience validation is performed at the composite level before any verifier is called; a token with a non-matching audience is immediately rejected (401) without consulting any verifier.' Update AC5's oracle to: 'a wrong-audience token causes the composite to immediately deny the request (observable: 401, no verifier's verify_token is called).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact string token identifier(s) for the strategies list (e.g. 'static' for StaticBearerVerifier) in the What section or the static_tokens schema, so the config→verifier factory has a specified dispatch vocabulary that subsequent stories can extend.
findings.finding: The strategies config key's valid value vocabulary is never specified: the plan says 'an ordered list selecting which verifiers are active' but never names the string tokens an operator may write (e.g. 'static', 'oidc', 'introspection'). The config→verifier factory is a named S2 deliverable, but without defined token identifiers, a factory implementer must invent the string vocabulary, creating an undocumented API surface that subsequent stories (S3-S6) must silently guess.
findings.location: What section ('strategies (ordered list selecting which verifiers are active)') / AC1 ('strategies')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line like: 'The strategies list accepts: "static" (StaticBearerVerifier, configured by static_tokens), with S3-S6 adding "oidc", "introspection", "proxy", "custom" respectively.' This makes the factory contract explicit and cross-story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical string vocabulary for the `strategies` list (e.g., `"static"` | `"jwt"` | `"introspection"` | `"trusted_proxy"` | `"custom"`) co-located with the `[mcp.auth]` config schema description, so the factory, TOML schema, env-var docs, and future stories all share a single normative reference.
findings.finding: The `strategies` config key is described as 'an ordered list selecting which verifiers are active' but its valid string value vocabulary — the identifiers an operator may place in the list — is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor building the config→verifier factory must invent the canonical strings (e.g., `"static"`, `"static_bearer"`, `"jwt"`, etc.) with no specification to bind to.
findings.location: What section — `[mcp.auth]` config group definition; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to 'What' enumerating the exact string identifiers and their one-line meaning, e.g.: `static` (StaticBearerVerifier), `jwt` (JWKS/JWT — S3), `introspection` (RFC 7662 — S4), `trusted_proxy` (S5), `custom` (S6). Reference this table in the factory spec and in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and name the exact method/attribute on `FastMCP` (or its module) that returns the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app for `httpx.ASGITransport` in the installed `mcp>=1.28` environment (e.g., `.streamable_http_app()`, `.asgi_app`, etc.), and add it to the integration-test AC as the concrete oracle.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 integration test uses 'the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete `mcp` SDK entrypoint)' with `httpx.ASGITransport`, implying a method like `streamable_http_app()` is accessible on the built `FastMCP` object. However, `streamable_http_app` is UNRESOLVED across all probed module paths (`mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp.server`, `mcp`). The current `mcp>=1.9` lower bound in `pyproject.toml` may not expose this API — and the plan does not specify the exact attribute/method name or import path for the ASGI app entrypoint the integration tests will use.
findings.location: AC2 — 'integration test (tier: integration — httpx `ASGITransport` against the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete `mcp` SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28,<2` at implementation time))'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and name the exact importable symbol/method for obtaining the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app from FastMCP at the target mcp version (e.g., `FastMCP.streamable_http_app()`, `mcp.server.streamable_http.app`, or similar), and add the confirmed symbol path to the AC2 test description.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `streamable_http_app()` as the ASGI entrypoint used in AC2's integration tests ('the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time'), but `streamable_http_app` is UNRESOLVED in the installed mcp environment under all plausible module paths.
findings.location: AC2 — 'httpx ASGITransport against the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact ASGI callable (e.g., `FastMCP.streamable_http_app()`, `mcp.server.streamable_http`, or equivalent) that will be passed to `httpx.ASGITransport(app=…)` in the integration tests, verified against the target SDK version.
findings.finding: The integration tests in AC lines 2–4 assert using `httpx.ASGITransport` against 'the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete `mcp` SDK entrypoint)'. The plan names this entry point but does not specify its exact callable — `streamable_http_app` is UNRESOLVED across all checked module paths in the installed SDK, and no usage of this symbol exists anywhere in the codebase or tests.
findings.location: AC — integration test bullet (tier: integration — httpx ASGITransport against the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit startup validation: if `token_env` is set but the named environment variable is absent or empty, treat it as a fatal misconfiguration (raise with a clear message), and add a corresponding AC unit-test oracle covering this case.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a token record omitting both `token_env` and `token_sha256` is rejected at startup, but does not address the case where `token_env` is set to a name that refers to an environment variable that is absent (unset or empty) at startup. This is a distinct failure mode that the schema description skips, leaving ambiguity about whether the server starts silently (ignoring the record), raises, or treats it as misconfiguration.
findings.location: What section — `static_tokens` schema paragraph; AC line: 'A record that embeds a plaintext token literal, or that omits both `token_env` and `token_sha256`, is rejected at startup'
findings.suggested_fix: In the `static_tokens` schema description, add: 'If `token_env` is set but the named environment variable is absent or evaluates to the empty string at startup, the server MUST raise a clear ConfigurationError (fail-closed), treating it identically to the omit-both-fields case.' Add an AC unit-test assertion: 'a record with `token_env` pointing to an unset variable raises at startup with a diagnostic message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (unit test) asserting that a `static_tokens` record whose `token_env` names a non-existent environment variable is rejected at startup with a clear error message (fail-closed guarantee for the missing-env-var case).
findings.finding: The static-token config spec defines three rejected cases (plaintext literal, missing both fields) but does not specify — and no AC tests — what happens when `token_env` names an environment variable that does not exist at startup. This is a realistic, distinct failure mode that could result in a silently unconfigured verifier or an uninformative runtime crash.
findings.location: AC line 5 / 'static_tokens schema' section / Security section
findings.suggested_fix: In the static-token schema section, add: 'A record whose token_env names an environment variable that is absent or empty at startup is also rejected (fail-closed).' Add a corresponding unit test oracle: startup raises with a message identifying the missing variable name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for a `token_env`-named variable that is absent or empty at startup: either (a) fail at startup with a clear error (recommended, consistent with the fail-closed posture) or (b) define the exact alternative behavior, co-located with the `static_tokens` schema description.
findings.finding: The fallback behavior when a `token_env`-sourced env var is absent or empty at startup is never specified. The plan states the verifier 'resolves the plaintext from `token_env`' and 'immediately stores only its SHA-256 digest', but does not define what happens when the named variable does not exist in the environment.
findings.location: What section — '`static_tokens` schema' paragraph; Security section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `static_tokens` schema paragraph: 'If `token_env` is specified but the named environment variable is absent or empty, startup fails with a ConfigError naming the missing variable and the token record's `client_id`.' This aligns with the Security section's 'refuse start' clause.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` (filtering `deprecated=False`) instead of the hardcoded three-var tuple, so new auth env vars automatically appear in the generated Gate-vars docs table.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 hardcodes a static tuple of exactly the three current env-var names (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`) for the 'Gate environment variables' docs table — it does NOT iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`. The plan directs adding new `REBAR_MCP_*` auth env vars to `MCP_ENV_VARS` (which correctly syncs `server.json` via the drift gate), but this unenumerated consumer means new auth vars will silently not appear in the generated `docs/mcp-reference.md` Gate-vars table after regeneration, even though all other machinery is updated.
findings.location: What section — 'Auto-derive `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars; update `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`'; AC1 — 'matching `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` (manifest drift gate passes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 208's `for var in ("REBAR_MCP_READONLY", "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM", "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC"):` with `for entry in [v for v in MCP_ENV_VARS if not v.get("deprecated")]: var = entry["name"]` (adjusting downstream `lines.append` accordingly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` as an edit target; change line 208's hardcoded tuple to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` so new auth vars are automatically included in the Gate environment variables table.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 hardcodes exactly the three current `REBAR_MCP_*` var names in the Gate environment variables table, rather than iterating `MCP_ENV_VARS`; adding new auth vars to `MCP_ENV_VARS` will silently omit them from the generated docs.
findings.location: AC1 — 'matching REBAR_MCP_* env vars in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json (manifest drift gate passes)'; `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `for var in ("REBAR_MCP_READONLY", "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM", "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC"):` with `for var in [v["name"] for v in env if not v.get("deprecated")]:` (where `env` is already the `MCP_ENV_VARS`-derived dict in scope).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docs-regeneration requirement to AC1: after updating `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, also regenerate `docs/mcp-reference.md` and `docs/env-vars.md` (or confirm which child story owns this for S2's auth config additions), to satisfy parent epic AC6.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC1 delivers only the MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json manifest drift gate for the new [mcp.auth] config keys, but omits the 'regenerated docs/mcp-reference.md / docs/env-vars.md' half that parent AC6 mandates for every story adding new config keys and env vars. No sibling story is identified as the owner of docs regeneration for the auth config additions (S7 is scoped to mcp-auth.md + ADR, not the reference docs).
findings.location: Leaf AC1 vs. Parent epic AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: '…and `docs/mcp-reference.md` / `docs/env-vars.md` are regenerated to include the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars (observable oracle: `make docs` or equivalent produces no diff).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (by reading the installed FastMCP source or upgrading pyproject.toml to `mcp>=1.28,<2`) that `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))` are valid constructor kwargs in the version the story will actually run against, and update the `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` floor accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan's two central wiring calls — `FastMCP(token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…), auth=AuthSettings(…))` — assert specific FastMCP constructor kwargs that are unconfirmed in the currently-installed SDK version. The plan defers resolution with 'resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time', but pyproject.toml currently floors at mcp>=1.9 with no upper bound, and the `token_verifier` and `auth` constructor kwargs are not confirmed to exist in the installed mcp environment.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire it in `build_server`: … pass `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…)` and `auth=AuthSettings(issuer_url=…, resource_server_url=…, required_scopes=…)` to `FastMCP`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `mcp>=1.28,<2` (or the minimum version that introduced `token_verifier` / `auth` kwargs) to `pyproject.toml`'s `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` and the `[project.optional-dependencies].dev` entries, and note the confirmed API shape in the plan so implementers don't need to re-discover it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify — against `FastMCP.__init__` in the pinned SDK version — that `token_verifier=` and `auth=` are accepted constructor kwargs, and update `pyproject.toml`'s `mcp>=1.9` lower bound to the minimum version where both params exist (the plan implies `>=1.28`).
findings.finding: The plan's two load-bearing auth-wiring calls — `FastMCP(token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…), auth=AuthSettings(…))` — assert specific constructor kwargs on `FastMCP` that cannot be verified against the installed SDK. `FastMCP` resolves but its constructor signature accepting `token_verifier=` and `auth=` is UNVERIFIED. The plan hedges 'at implementation time' with `mcp>=1.28,<2`, but pyproject.toml's lower bound remains `mcp>=1.9`, meaning CI/dev environments could run a version that lacks these params.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire it in `build_server`'; AC line 2 (401 test); AC line 4 (200/401/403 test)
findings.suggested_fix: Bump `mcp = ["mcp>=1.28,<2"]` in `[project.optional-dependencies]` (and matching `dev` entry) to match the plan's stated implementation-time bound, then add a `resolve_symbol`-level smoke test or `inspect.signature` assertion in the integration test setup to fail early with a clear message if the SDK version is too old.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (against the target SDK version's source or docs) that passing `auth=AuthSettings(issuer_url=…)` to `FastMCP` causes the SDK to automatically serve the RFC 9728 PRM document at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` with an `authorization_servers` field, and name the SDK version where this behavior was introduced.
findings.finding: The AC asserts the PRM document is automatically served at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` with an `authorization_servers` field when `auth=AuthSettings(…)` is passed to `FastMCP`. Whether the SDK auto-registers this route and populates `authorization_servers` from `AuthSettings.issuer_url` is UNVERIFIED — this is a specific endpoint surface on a new SDK capability path.
findings.location: AC — 'Integration test asserts the PRM document is served at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` with an `authorization_servers` field'

findings.finding: The Security section states 'fail-closed (verifier error or misconfigured auth → deny / refuse start)' but no Acceptance Criterion or test covers the 'refuse start' / startup-validation failure path — e.g., `mcp.auth.enabled=true` with an empty or internally inconsistent strategy list, or a static-token entry missing required fields. This is a testable security-critical failure path with no observable oracle in the ACs.
findings.location: Security section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC present for startup misconfiguration)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Unit/integration test asserts that constructing `CompositeTokenVerifier` with an empty verifier list, or `build_server` with `mcp.auth.enabled=true` and no valid strategy, raises a `ConfigurationError` (or equivalent) at startup — not a silent denial at request time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the config→verifier factory: at minimum, that an unknown `strategies` entry is rejected at startup (fail-closed), and that a valid config correctly instantiates the StaticBearerVerifier with the right scopes/client_id (observable oracle: startup error message or verifier instance attributes).
findings.finding: The config→verifier factory is explicitly named as a deliverable in 'What' ('Plus a config→verifier **factory**') but no acceptance criterion verifies its existence, correctness, or behavior. It is an orphan deliverable.
findings.location: 'What' section (factory mention) vs. Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the composite verifier's fail-closed error contract, mandatory audience validation (SDK override), and SHA-256 hash-at-rest pattern in a persistent decision record (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design doc) so future verifier implementors and auditors have a durable reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces novel architectural decisions (composite fail-closed contract, mandatory audience validation not performed by the SDK, SHA-256 hash-at-rest pattern for static tokens) that cross the security boundary, but does not specify capturing these decisions in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section; 'Mandatory audience validation' bullet in What
findings.suggested_fix: Add a task to the story: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or create docs/adr/0002-mcp-auth-framework.md) documenting: (1) the fail-closed raise semantics vs swallow-to-None, (2) mandatory audience validation as an intentional SDK override, (3) SHA-256-at-rest rationale.' The story's Approach & rationale prose can be copied and lightly edited as the ADR body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that composite verifier errors are emitted at ERROR log level with structured fields (verifier class name, exception type/message) and recommend a counter metric increment, with an AC assertion that a raising verifier produces a detectable log/metric artifact.
findings.finding: The plan states that when a verifier raises an exception the composite 'logs it and immediately fails closed,' but specifies no structured observability artifact (log level, structured field, metric counter, or alert hook) for this event. In production, silent log-level errors for every request denial would be invisible to operators without explicit instrumentation.
findings.location: What section — `_mcp_auth.py` module description: 'a verifier that raises is an internal error — the composite logs it and immediately fails closed (denies)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `_mcp_auth.py` spec: 'When a verifier raises, log at ERROR with fields: verifier=<class name>, exc_type=<exception type> (never include the token value). Increment a `mcp_auth_verifier_errors_total{verifier=...}` counter if a metrics registry is available.' Add an AC assertion: 'unit test asserts that a raising verifier causes an ERROR-level log entry with the verifier class name (observable oracle: log capture).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `strategies` config key is needed at S2 or can be deferred: if only `StaticBearerVerifier` is registered at delivery, consider making `strategies` an internal implementation detail of the factory (hardcoded at S2, promoted to a user-facing config key at S3 when a second verifier exists and the ordering is actually meaningful).
findings.finding: The `strategies` ordered-list config key is premature at S2: with only one verifier (`StaticBearerVerifier`) in scope, the key's ordering and selection logic has exactly one valid value (`["static"]`) and its multi-verifier dispatch behaviour cannot be exercised by any S2 test. It is a config surface exposed to operators before any second consumer exists, violating YAGNI and the Rule-of-Three for call-sites.
findings.location: What section — '[mcp.auth] group: strategies (ordered list selecting which verifiers are active)'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: At S2, the factory can unconditionally instantiate `StaticBearerVerifier` when `mcp.auth.enabled=true` and `static_tokens` is non-empty, without exposing a `strategies` list. Introduce `strategies` as a user-facing config key at S3 when there are at least two registered verifier types and the ordering has observable, testable effect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the TLS/transport-encryption posture explicitly in the Security section: either (a) require TLS termination before the server accepts connections (and document it as an operator requirement with a startup warning when `mcp.auth.enabled=true` and no HTTPS is detectable), or (b) explicitly state the assumed deployment model (TLS-at-proxy, e.g. nginx/Caddy in front) and document it as a prerequisite in the plan and any operator guide.
findings.finding: The plan introduces bearer-token authentication on an HTTP endpoint reachable by lower-trust actors but never declares a TLS/HTTPS requirement or posture for the MCP server itself. Bearer tokens sent over plaintext HTTP are trivially intercepted and replayed, making the entire token-security model (SHA-256 hash, constant-time compare, audience validation) irrelevant if the transport is unencrypted.
findings.location: Security section; AC2 (401 + WWW-Authenticate test); AC4 (valid token → 200)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'Transport security: when `mcp.auth.enabled=true`, the server MUST be deployed behind a TLS-terminating proxy (nginx, Caddy, or equivalent); `build_server` logs a WARNING at startup if the transport is HTTP and auth is enabled (detectable via the configured `resource_server_url` scheme or an explicit `mcp.auth.require_tls` flag, default true). Plain HTTP with auth enabled is a misconfiguration, not a supported mode.' This makes the transport posture an explicit, operator-visible contract rather than an undeclared assumption.

findings.finding: The format/structure of the `static_tokens` source is never specified — the plan says only 'static-token source' in config, but doesn't define whether this is a TOML inline list of token records, a file path to a secrets file, per-token env vars, or a secret-store URI. An executor cannot implement `StaticBearerVerifier` loading or write the config AC without this decision.
findings.location: What section, bullet 1 (`static_tokens` source); AC 1 (config exists); AC 4 (valid static token → 200)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the What section: `static_tokens` is a TOML array-of-tables, each entry having `token_hash` (lowercase hex SHA-256 of the raw bearer value), `client_id` (string), and `scopes` (list of strings). The raw token is never written to config; callers pre-hash it or use the provided CLI helper (S7). Example:
```toml
[[mcp.auth.static_tokens]]
token_hash = "e3b0c44298fc1c149afb..."
client_id = "ci-runner"
scopes = ["read"]
```

findings.finding: The `static_tokens` config field is named and its per-token attributes (`client_id`, `scopes`) are mentioned in the Security section, but the schema of each token entry is never defined: the plan does not specify whether this is a TOML array-of-tables (`[[mcp.auth.static_tokens]]`), an inline list, what fields are required vs. optional, or how the plaintext token value is supplied (env reference, inline string, file path) before being hashed at rest. The `StaticBearerVerifier` implementation depends on this schema, as does the test that verifies no plaintext is retained.
findings.location: What section — `static_tokens source`; Security section — 'only a SHA-256 hash of each static token is held in memory'; AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema block to the What section, e.g.: each entry is a TOML inline-table `{token = "<plaintext-or-env-ref>", client_id = "<string>", scopes = ["<scope1>", ...]}`. Specify that `token` is consumed once at startup (hashed, plaintext discarded), `client_id` and `scopes` are required.

findings.finding: The `static_tokens` source field is named but the schema of each individual token entry is never defined. The What section mentions 'per-token `client_id`/`scopes`' only in passing; the plan never specifies the full field list (plaintext token value, client_id, scopes, optional audience override, etc.) or types that the `StaticBearerVerifier` must deserialize from config.
findings.location: What section — 'the static-verifier params (`static_tokens` source)'; 'per-token `client_id`/`scopes`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Static token entry schema' subsection under What, e.g.: each entry is a TOML inline-table `{token = "…", client_id = "…", scopes = ["…"]}`. The verifier hashes `token` on load and discards the plaintext; `client_id` and `scopes` populate the returned `AccessToken`; audience defaults to the global `resource_server_url` unless overridden per-entry.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
